pred_reason: The labels SWC-101 and SWC-107 are reasonable because the contract exhibits integer overflow/underflow vulnerabilities (SWC-101) in arithmetic operations and improper authorization checks (SWC-107) in privileged functions. These vulnerabilities were identified across multiple functions in the audit findings. Evidence: Unchecked arithmetic operations in depositAndLock and dispersePaymentForShares functions; Insufficient access controls in setBlockClaimsOperator and submitClaim functions; Broken payment calculation due to improper arithmetic handling

The labels SWC-101 (Integer Overflow and Underflow) and SWC-107 (Reentrancy) are reasonable due to identified integer overflow risks in totalSupply calculations and multiple reentrancy vulnerabilities in the Release function. Evidence: Potential overflow in totalSupply() when multiplying large WETH balances by 1 billion (SWC-101); Release function makes external calls before state changes, violating Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern (SWC-107); Cross-function reentrancy possibilities between Release() and EncryptedSwap() functions (SWC-107)

SWC-101 is relevant due to the reentrancy vulnerability in _transfer() violating checks-effects-interactions pattern. SWC-107 applies because the contract falsely claims renounced ownership while maintaining privileged functions like unlimited minting. Evidence: Reentrancy risk in _transfer() violates CEI pattern (SWC-101); Contract maintains minting capability despite 'Renounced Ownership' claims (SWC-107); Inconsistent ownership updates between functions contradict decentralization claims
